description: The single brand color. Used for primary CTA backgrounds (Reserve, Continue), the search orb, the heart save state on property cards, and inline brand links. The most recognizable color in consumer travel.
The press / pointer-down variant — slightly more saturated. Used on `{component.button-primary-active}`.
A pale tint used on disabled CTAs.
Sub-brand accent for Airbnb Luxe. Only appears inside Luxe-branded surfaces — never in mainline marketing.
Sub-brand accent for Airbnb Plus. Same scoping as Luxe — sub-product only.
The default page floor for every public page. Airbnb does not have a dark mode on the public web.
The lightest fill — used on disabled fields, sub-nav hover backgrounds, and the inline search filter band.
Slightly heavier fill — circular icon-button surface (e.g., the breadcrumb back-arrow and listing toolbar buttons).
The default 1px border tone — search bar dividers, table separators, footer column splitters, card 1px borders.
A lighter divider used on long-scrolling editorial body separators.
A heavier stroke used on disabled outline buttons and form input outlines after focus.
The dominant text color on light surfaces. Display headlines, body paragraphs, primary nav links, and most inline link text. Never pure black.
A secondary running-text color used inside long-form review and amenity copy where ink would feel too heavy.
Sub-titles inside city link blocks ("Cottage rentals", "Villa rentals"), inactive product-tab labels, footer category sub-labels, "View all" links.
Disabled link text. Used very sparingly.
The same ink token — Airbnb's star icon and "4.81" rating numbers all render in ink rather than a yellow/gold color, which is a deliberate brand choice (yellow stars feel cheap in travel context).
White text on Rausch CTAs.
Inline error text for form validation. Distinct from Rausch — slightly darker, more saturated red.
Darkens on link hover.
Inline links inside legal copy (Privacy, Terms). Only used inside the legal sub-band.
The global modal backdrop tone — date picker, login dialog, language picker. Stored as the base hex; opacity is applied at render time.

The dominant brand color. Used for the primary CTA background, h1/h2 display type, and the `{component.surface-dark}` band. Not "blue, then black" — black IS the primary throughout the marketing system.
The press state on primary buttons.
The default page surface; the floor of every editorial body.
Tabbed feature cards and the featured pricing tier.
The light gray "Start building with Airtable" CTA banner near the footer.
The dark navy CTA cards used mid-page (for example "The path to 10× every person in your organization").
The articles-page hero base behind the rainbow-stripe overlay.
The 1px border tone for input outlines, table dividers, secondary-button outlines.
The strongest text — h1/h2 display type and primary button text-on-light. Same hex as `{colors.primary}` because they are the same role expressed at type and button layers.
The default running-text color.
Footer links, breadcrumbs, captions.
The 1px outline color on disabled secondary buttons.
The text color on primary buttons and dark surfaces.
The largest signature card on the homepage ("Production apps in prototype speed"). Full-bleed dark coral with white type.
A deep-green signature card used in the homepage demo-grid cluster.
The cream callout band ("The path to 10× every person in your organization") — a soft beige surface holding dark type and product UI fragments.
Demo-card surfaces that carry small product UI fragments inside the multi-card grid sections.

Inline body links and anchor text. Darker on press to `{colors.link-active}` (#1a3866). Despite the `--theme_button-background-primary` CSS-variable name, this color is **not** the primary button color — it is the link color.
Inline info badges and focused-input outline.
Confirmation states.

The single brand-level interactive color. All text links, all blue pill CTAs ("Learn more", "Buy"), and the focus ring root. This is Apple's quiet but universal "click me" signal. Press state shifts to a slightly darker variant via the active scale transform rather than a hex change.
A marginally brighter sibling of Action Blue, reserved for the keyboard focus ring on buttons (`outline: 2px solid`).
A brighter blue used on dark surfaces for in-copy links and inline callouts, where Action Blue would disappear against the tile background.
The dominant canvas. Content, utility cards, store tiles, configurator grids.
The signature Apple off-white. Used for alternating light tiles, footer region, and the default page canvas in store utility sections. Just different enough from white to create rhythm.
A near-white used as the fill for secondary "ghost" buttons — lighter than the parchment canvas so the button still reads as a button against `{colors.canvas-parchment}`.
The primary dark-tile surface on the homepage product grid.
A micro-step lighter — used where a dark tile sits directly above or below Tile 1 to create the faintest separation.
A micro-step darker — used at the bottom of the stack and in embedded video/player frames.
Reserved for true void — video player backgrounds, edge-to-edge photographic overlays, the global nav bar background.
The base hex of the translucent gray chip used over photography for circular control buttons. In production, applied at ~64% alpha as `rgba(210, 210, 215, 0.64)`.
The voice of every headline, every body paragraph, and the dark utility button's fill. Chosen instead of pure black to keep the page feeling photographic rather than printed.
Same hex as ink — Apple uses one near-black tone for all text on light surfaces.
All text on dark tiles and on the global nav bar.
Secondary copy on dark tiles where pure white would be too loud.
Body text on the white Pearl Button surface — slightly softer than pure black.
Disabled button text and legal fine-print.
The "border" tone on secondary buttons — functions as a ring shadow rather than a hard line. In production, often applied as `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.04)`.
The 1px hairline border on store utility cards and configurator chips.
The single brand action color. All primary CTAs, "Learn More" link prefixes (blue text), nav-link active state. Press shifts to `{colors.primary-active}` (#0653b6).
The M tricolor stripe — appears on the corporate site only on M-model pages and the "M" badge. Never as CTA colors.
The default page surface.
Soft grey for the footer and sub-navigation bands.
The light plate behind a model card's photo block.
A slightly heavier grey used as a section divider.
Dark navy for hero bands and large dark CTAs. Not pure black — carries a warm undertone.
One step lighter, used for nested cards on top of the dark hero.
The 1px divider — input outline, configurator card outline, table separator.
A more visible 1px outline — disabled secondary buttons, emphasized table border.
All display and primary text. Not pure black — soft against photography.
Default running text.
Emphasized paragraphs and lead text.
Footer links, breadcrumbs, captions.
Disabled text, fine-print legal.
White text on a blue button.
White text on a dark hero band.
A slightly muted white for secondary text on dark bands.
Confirmation messages and "available" indicators.
Warning callouts.
Validation errors.
The dominant action color. All primary CTAs, h1/h2 display type. Press state shifts to `{colors.primary-active}` (#242424).
Used sparely on inline links and on a small badge / "Customer story" highlight. Cal.com is a near-monochrome brand — the blue appears rarely.

`{colors.badge-orange}` (#fb923c), `{colors.badge-pink}` (#ec4899), `{colors.badge-violet}` (#8b5cf6), `{colors.badge-emerald}` (#34d399). These appear on tag pills and small accent moments inside product UI fragments — never on hero CTAs.
The default page floor.
Nav-pill-group background, very-soft section dividers.
Feature cards, testimonial cards, badge pills, default avatar fills.
Hairline border alternative; disabled button background.

Used for nested cards inside the dark footer or featured pricing card.
The 1px border tone on light surfaces. Used on input borders, table dividers, content card outlines (sometimes).
A barely-visible divider used between sections that share the white canvas.

Used sparingly on secondary product surfaces (terminal status indicators, "active connection" dots in connectors page).

Barely-visible divider used inside the same band.

The signature accent color — a warm, saturated amber-gold (rgb 255, 192, 0) used exclusively for primary action buttons ("Discover More", "Tickets", "Start Configuration"). The only chromatic color in the entire interface, it ignites against the black canvas like a headlight cutting through night
Primary text color on dark surfaces, logo rendering, nav elements, and light-mode button fills — the voice that speaks from the darkness
Hover/pressed state for gold buttons — a deep amber (rgb 145, 115, 0) that darkens the gold to signal interaction
Slightly lighter gold variant (rgb 255, 206, 62) used for inline text accents and highlighted labels
Electric blue-cyan (rgb 41, 171, 226) appearing as an informational accent and interactive element highlight
Medium blue (rgb 56, 96, 190) used universally for link hover states across all text colors
The dominant surface color — used for page background, hero sections, header, footer, and most containers
Elevated dark surface (rgb 32, 32, 32) — the primary "dark gray" for cards, panels, and text containers sitting above the black canvas
Subtle surface variant (rgb 24, 24, 24) — barely distinguishable from black, used for footer and deep sections

Rare light surface (rgb 248, 248, 248) for content blocks in white-mode sections
Light gray surface for secondary light-mode containers

Reserved for the highest-emphasis moments — ghost button text and maximum-contrast headings. Used at low opacity (5%) for subtle overlay effects.

Tertiary text, metadata, timestamps, and de-emphasized content. A cool blue-gray that provides clear hierarchy below Warm Parchment.
Footer links and supporting navigation text — brightens on hover to Pure White.
Slightly brighter than Fog, used for secondary link text that transitions to bright green on hover.
Highest-contrast secondary text, one step below Snow White.
Deep green for success states and positive confirmations in documentation contexts.
Soft pastel green for success backgrounds and subtle positive indicators.
Bright amber for warning alerts and caution states.
Softened amber for warning background fills.
Vivid red for error states and destructive action warnings.
Softened coral-pink for error backgrounds.
Cool teal-blue for informational callouts and tip admonitions.
A muted blue-gray used exclusively for decorative dashed borders in workflow diagrams.

`drop-shadow(0 0 2px #00d992)` animating to `drop-shadow(0 0 8px #00d992)` — creates a pulsing "electric charge" effect on the VoltAgent bolt logo and interactive elements. The glow expands and contracts like a heartbeat.
`rgba(92, 88, 85, 0.2) 0px 0px 15px` — a warm-toned diffused shadow that creates a soft atmospheric glow around elevated cards, visible at the edges without sharp boundaries.

`rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.7) 0px 20px 60px` with `rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.1) 0px 0px 0px 1px inset` — a heavy, dramatic downward shadow paired with a faint inset slate ring for the most prominent floating elements.

Button backgrounds, dark interactive surfaces — warm, not cold

The singular text color, link color, and all foreground elements. In xAI's system, white is not a background -- it is the voice.
The canvas. A warm near-black with a subtle blue undertone (not pure black, not neutral gray). This specific hue prevents the harsh eye strain of `#000000` while maintaining deep darkness.
Link and interactive element color in default state.
Hover state for links -- a deliberate dimming rather than brightening, which is unusual and distinctive.
Borders, dividers, and subtle surface treatments.
Tailwind's default focus ring color (`--tw-ring-color`), used for keyboard accessibility focus states.
Subtle card backgrounds and hover surfaces -- barely visible lift.
Slightly more visible hover state for interactive containers.
Standard border for cards, dividers, and containers.
Emphasized borders for active states and button outlines.
All headings, body text, labels.
Descriptions, captions, supporting text.
Muted labels, placeholder text, timestamps.
Disabled text, very subtle annotations.
Primary text, headings, dark button backgrounds. A warm near-black with reddish undertones -- never cold.
Page background, card surfaces, light button fills. Not pure white; the yellowish warmth is intentional.
Secondary background surface, subtle alternate tint. Nearly indistinguishable from cream white but creates depth.
Primary CTA buttons, active underline indicators, accent borders. The signature color -- vivid and warm.
Secondary text, footer text, border color for strong dividers. A warm dark gray-brown with 70% opacity variant.
Tertiary text, muted labels, timestamp-style content. Mid-range with greenish-warm undertone.
Primary border color, hover state backgrounds, divider lines. The backbone of Zapier's structural elements.
Secondary button backgrounds, light borders, subtle card surfaces.
Alternate border tone, used on specific span elements.

Secondary dark buttons with sand hover state.
Tertiary/ghost buttons with sand hover.
Standard link color, matching body text.
Links remove `text-decoration: underline` on hover (inverse pattern).
Overlay button variant, backdrop-like elements.
